skill.name: modern-javascript-patterns
skill.description: Modernize JavaScript with ES6+ syntax, async patterns, modules, iterators, generators, and functional programming techniques for cleaner applications.
skill.summary: Guide JavaScript refactors and implementation work with practical ES6+ patterns and maintainable async code.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found dynamic imports, fetch calls, URLs, and many Markdown backtick matches. Review shows this skill is documentation with JavaScript examples, no executable install scripts, no command execution, no prompt injection, and no evidence of data exfiltration. A low-risk warning remains because examples include network requests and a toy password hashing method that users should not copy into production.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Include Network Requests
low_findings.description: The fetch calls and documentation links are teaching examples and reference URLs, not hidden runtime behavior. They are low risk because the skill contains Markdown only, but generated code should still be reviewed before production use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown examples and resource links. No executable wrapper, credential handling, or external collection endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Toy Password Hashing Example
low_findings.description: The class example prefixes a password with a string and calls it hashed. This is not malicious, but it could teach an unsafe password-storage pattern if copied directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is clearly part of a class feature example, not active credential processing. The security weakness is still concrete because the example names it password hashing.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown code fences, inline paths, and JavaScript template literals. They do not execute shell commands in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation syntax or JavaScript template literals inside Markdown. No Ruby file, shell script, child process call, or command invocation path was present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography alerts point to destructuring headings, destructuring examples, and a best-practice bullet. These lines do not contain cryptographic algorithms or active hashing code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation text and variable destructuring examples. I found no MD5, SHA1, cipher use, key handling, or active crypto operation at those locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to generic example variables, loop identifiers, and best-practice text. No code reads hostnames, environment details, process lists, or system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are JavaScript examples and prose about immutability or blocking operations. No system inventory, shell probing, or host-specific collection behavior was present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Import Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import expressions load local example modules in documentation. They are not active scripts, so the static script findings are false positives for marketplace execution risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import expressions are inside a Markdown code block under the ES6 modules section. The imported paths are local example files, not attacker-controlled strings or remote URLs.

content.value_statement: Legacy JavaScript can become hard to read and difficult to maintain. This skill guides refactors with practical ES6+ patterns and async best practices.
actual_capabilities: Explains ES6+ syntax such as destructuring, spread, rest, template literals, and enhanced object literals.
Guides migration from callbacks and promise chains to async and await patterns.
Shows functional patterns using map, filter, reduce, composition, currying, and pure functions.
Covers modules, dynamic imports, iterators, generators, classes, and modern operators.
Provides refactoring guidance for readable, maintainable, and performance-aware JavaScript.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run tests or inspect projects by itself.
Examples are generic and may need adaptation for browser, Node.js, or framework-specific constraints.
It does not replace security review for authentication, cryptography, or production data handling.
It focuses on JavaScript patterns, not TypeScript type design or framework architecture.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy Frontend Code
use_cases.description: Convert older syntax into clearer ES6+ code while keeping behavior stable.

use_cases.title: Improve Async Data Flows
use_cases.description: Replace nested callbacks or long promise chains with structured async and await handling.

use_cases.title: Teach Modern JavaScript Practices
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain modern language features and common pitfalls.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner learning a specific ES6+ feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain JavaScript destructuring in simple terms. Include when to use it and one small before-and-after example.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer modernizing a small code section.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this JavaScript function using modern ES6+ patterns. Preserve behavior and explain each meaningful change.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Async Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Team replacing callback or promise-chain logic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this async JavaScript flow. Convert it to async and await, add error handling, and note any concurrency tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Pattern Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a modern JavaScript pattern guide for this codebase. Cover modules, immutability, async boundaries, and performance risks.

output_examples.input: Refactor a callback-based data loader into modern JavaScript.
output_examples.output: A cleaner async and await version with explicit error handling, parallel loading where safe, and notes about changed control flow.

output_examples.input: Explain when to use map, filter, and reduce.
output_examples.output: A short comparison that maps each method to transformation, selection, or aggregation, with guidance on readability tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Review a module design that uses dynamic imports.
output_examples.output: A recommendation for local module boundaries, lazy loading conditions, and validation points before using dynamic imports in production.

best_practices: Ask for the target runtime before recommending syntax that may need transpilation or polyfills.
Preserve behavior during refactors and identify each change that could affect execution order.
Review generated examples for project security rules before using them in production.
anti_patterns: Do not modernize syntax just to reduce lines if the result is harder to maintain.
Do not replace sequential async code with parallel execution when order or rate limits matter.
Do not copy toy authentication or password examples into production systems.

faq.question: Does this skill execute JavaScript code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for modern JavaScript patterns.

faq.question: Can it help migrate callbacks to async and await?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for promises, async functions, retry logic, and error handling.

faq.question: Does it support browser and Node.js projects?
faq.answer: Yes, but examples may need runtime-specific changes for APIs, modules, and build tools.

faq.question: Can it review production security code?
faq.answer: It can flag obvious concerns, but it is not a replacement for focused security review.

faq.question: Is TypeScript covered?
faq.answer: Only indirectly. The skill focuses on JavaScript language patterns rather than TypeScript type modeling.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it for tasks unrelated to JavaScript patterns, framework architecture, or build-system configuration.
